prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a small determined robot balancing on a wobbly pole atop a rolling cart, mid practice run, while ghostly faded copies of its earlier crashed attempts pile up softly behind it like a trail of brave failures, the robot grinning with fresh resolve. Kurzgesagt meets XKCD, expressive body language, one clear visual gag, generous negative space, warm colors. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A robot balancing a pole on a cart while faded copies of its past failed attempts pile up behind it, learning by repeated trial and error.
caption: Deep reinforcement learning learns the only way it knows how, by failing in a thousand small ways until balance becomes a habit.

after_heading: 2. The Two Stabilizers: Experience Replay and a Target Network
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a calm robot student studying from a big shuffled shoebox of memory snapshots it pulls out at random, while across the desk a slower second robot holds perfectly still as a frozen reference statue for the student to aim at. The contrast between the busy shuffling learner and the patient frozen twin is the gag. Warm colors, expressive faces, generous negative space, XKCD feel. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A robot learner drawing random memory snapshots from a shuffled box while a frozen twin robot holds still as a steady reference target.
caption: Replay breaks the spell of correlated experience by shuffling the past, and a frozen target twin gives the learner something steady to aim at.

after_heading: 2. The Policy Gradient Theorem and REINFORCE
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a blindfolded robot archer cheerfully firing a wild scatter of arrows in every direction at a distant target, a few landing close by sheer luck, its expression hopeful and oblivious to the chaos. The wide spray of arrows dramatizes noisy high variance learning. Warm colors, expressive pose, one clear gag, generous negative space, Kurzgesagt style. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A blindfolded robot archer happily firing a wild scatter of arrows toward a target, only a few landing near it, dramatizing high variance policy gradients.
caption: Plain REINFORCE points in the right direction on average, but every single update is a noisy shot that can land almost anywhere.

after_heading: 3. Variance Reduction: Baselines and the Advantage Function
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: two robots being judged on a small podium, but instead of raw scores the judge compares each one against a calm gray average robot standing on a center line, lifting the above average performer up and gently lowering the below average one. The comparison against the steady baseline figure is the visual point. Warm colors, expressive faces, generous negative space, XKCD feel. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A judge comparing two robots against a steady gray average robot on a center line, raising the better one and lowering the worse, showing advantage as a baseline subtraction.
caption: Subtracting a baseline asks not how good an action was but how much better than usual, which is a far quieter and more useful signal.

after_heading: 1. Actor-Critic: A Policy and a Critic That Cuts Variance
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: an energetic robot performer dancing boldly on a small stage while a thoughtful seated robot critic in the front row gives an instant approving or skeptical thumbs gesture after each move, the performer adjusting its next step from that feedback. The tight feedback loop between the doer and the appraiser is the gag. Warm colors, expressive faces, generous negative space, Kurzgesagt style. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: An energetic robot performer dancing on stage while a seated robot critic gives an instant thumbs reaction after each move, illustrating the actor and critic feedback loop.
caption: The actor acts and the critic judges, and because the verdict arrives every single step the actor never has to guess how it is doing.

after_heading: 2. PPO: The Clipped Surrogate That Became the Default
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: an eager robot on roller skates trying to lunge far forward toward a glowing goal, held back by a short gentle elastic tether anchored to where it started, so it can only inch ahead a safe amount each step instead of flying off and tumbling. The short leash that prevents overcommitting is the visual point. Warm colors, expressive pose, generous negative space, XKCD feel. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: An eager robot on skates lunging toward a glowing goal but held to small safe steps by a short elastic tether to its starting point, illustrating PPO clipping and trust regions.
caption: PPO lets the policy improve only a cautious step at a time, because lunging too far on one noisy update is how training quietly falls apart.

after_heading: 1. Continuous Action Spaces: Why DQN Does Not Apply
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a puzzled robot standing between two choices, on one side a few big chunky buttons it can press, on the other a long smooth control dial it must turn to any exact position, its hand hovering uncertainly over the infinite smoothness of the dial. The contrast between a handful of buttons and a continuous dial is the gag. Warm colors, expressive face, generous negative space, Kurzgesagt style. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A puzzled robot choosing between a few chunky buttons and a smooth continuous dial it must set to any exact position, showing why discrete action methods fail on continuous control.
caption: When actions are a smooth dial rather than a few buttons, asking which discrete choice is best stops making sense and you must learn to set the dial directly.

after_heading: 1. Why Deep RL Is Brittle: Variance, Sensitivity, and the Reproducibility Crisis
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: five identical robots given the same recipe each cooking the same dish at the same time, yet pulling wildly different results from their ovens, one a tall cake, one a flat pancake, one a smoking lump, all shrugging in confusion at how the very same steps diverged. The same setup yielding wildly different outcomes is the gag. Warm colors, expressive faces, generous negative space, XKCD feel. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: Five identical robots following the same recipe yet pulling wildly different dishes from their ovens, one a cake, one a pancake, one a burnt lump, illustrating deep RL run to run variance.
caption: Run the same deep RL recipe a few times and you can get triumph, mediocrity, and disaster, which is why a single lucky seed proves almost nothing.
